Description: Group Policy Object health and consistency checks

Checks.PassMessage: No orphaned or inconsistent GPOs detected

EvaluationRules.Message: GPO exists in Active Directory but not in SYSVOL
GPO folder exists in SYSVOL but not in Active Directory
GPO version numbers differ between AD and SYSVOL
Empty unlinked GPO detected - cleanup candidate
GPO issue detected
GPO health check reported failure

Checks.Help: Detects GPOs that exist in AD but not SYSVOL, or vice versa. Orphaned GPOs indicate incomplete GPO deletion or SYSVOL replication failures and can cause policy application errors.
Checks.Remediation: 1. For AD-only orphans: delete via GPMC or Remove-GPO cmdlet. 2. For SYSVOL-only orphans: manually delete the GUID folder from SYSVOL\Policies on all DCs. 3. Investigate SYSVOL replication health if many orphans exist: dfsrdiag ReplicationState.

Checks.PassMessage: All GPO versions are consistent between AD and SYSVOL

EvaluationRules.Message: GPO user policy version mismatch between AD and SYSVOL
GPO computer policy version mismatch between AD and SYSVOL
GPO replication inconsistency detected

Checks.Help: Checks that GPO version numbers are consistent between the AD object (GPC) and the SYSVOL folder (GPT). Version mismatches mean clients may apply outdated policy settings.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Force SYSVOL replication: dfsrdiag SyncNow /partner:<DC> /RGName:'Domain System Volume'. 2. Check DFSR replication health: Get-DfsrBacklog -DestinationComputerName <DC>. 3. If a specific GPO is persistently mismatched, delete and recreate it. 4. Review DFSR event log for replication errors.

Checks.PassMessage: SYSVOL GPO content is consistent across all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: SYSVOL inconsistencies detected across domain controllers

EvaluationRules.Message: SYSVOL GPO content inconsistent across domain controllers
SYSVOL consistency issue detected

Checks.Help: Verifies that SYSVOL content (scripts, GPO templates) is consistent across all domain controllers. SYSVOL inconsistency causes clients to receive different policy depending on which DC they authenticate against.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Run: dfsrdiag ReplicationState on each DC to check DFSR sync status. 2. Force full SYSVOL sync: Set-DfsrServiceConfiguration -ComputerName <DC> -GlobalSettings. 3. If DFSR is not used (older domains), check FRS replication: ntfrsutl ds <DC>. 4. In severe cases, perform a non-authoritative or authoritative SYSVOL restore.

Checks.PassMessage: All GPO permissions are correctly configured

EvaluationRules.Message: One or more GPOs have permission configuration issues
GPO permission issue detected

Checks.Help: Verifies that GPO permissions allow Authenticated Users to read and apply policy. Incorrect permissions can prevent GPOs from applying to intended users and computers.
Checks.Remediation: 1. In GPMC, right-click the GPO and check Delegation tab. 2. Ensure 'Authenticated Users' has Read and Apply Group Policy permissions. 3. If using security filtering, ensure the target group has Apply permission. 4. Run: Get-GPPermission -Name '<GPOName>' -All to review permissions via PowerShell.

Checks.PassMessage: All WMI filters are valid and linked to active GPOs

EvaluationRules.Message: Orphaned WMI filters detected - not linked to any GPO (cleanup candidates)
WMI filter issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks for WMI filters attached to GPOs and validates their syntax. Invalid or orphaned WMI filters waste resources and filters with syntax errors prevent GPO application.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Open GPMC and review GPOs listed as having WMI filters. 2. Test WMI filters manually: Get-WmiObject -Query '<WQL>' on a target machine. 3. Delete orphaned WMI filters via ADSI Edit: CN=SOM,CN=WMIPolicy,CN=System,DC=... 4. Fix syntax errors - WMI filters require valid WQL with SELECT and FROM clauses.

EvaluationRules.Message: One or more GPOs have no settings (Computer and User DSVersion both 0)
Empty GPO detected - review and remove if no longer needed

Checks.Help: Detects GPOs that have no computer or user settings configured (both version counters are zero). Empty GPOs waste processing time during Group Policy refresh on all affected clients.
Checks.Remediation: 1. In GPMC identify empty GPOs (no settings icon in the details view). 2. Either add required settings to the GPO or delete it. 3. Before deleting, verify the GPO is not linked anywhere: right-click the GPO and check Links. 4. Delete via GPMC or: Remove-GPO -Name '<GPOName>' -Domain <domain>.

EvaluationRules.Message: One or more GPO links are disabled (LinkOption=1) - cleanup candidates
One or more GPO links are disabled and enforced (LinkOption=3)
Disabled GPO link detected - review and remove if no longer needed

Checks.Help: Finds GPO links that have been disabled at the link level. Disabled links are often left after troubleshooting and represent cleanup debt. They do not apply policy but still appear in GPMC causing confusion.
Checks.Remediation: 1. In GPMC, navigate to each OU with disabled links (shown with a red X). 2. Right-click the disabled link and either re-enable it or delete it. 3. If the link was disabled intentionally, add a comment explaining why. 4. Via PowerShell: Set-GPLink -Name '<GPO>' -Target '<OU DN>' -LinkEnabled Yes or remove the link.

Checks.PassMessage: No OUs with blocked GPO inheritance detected
Checks.FailMessage: OUs with blocked GPO inheritance detected

EvaluationRules.Message: One or more OUs have GPO inheritance blocked - verify this is intentional
Blocked GPO inheritance detected

Checks.Help: Identifies OUs where Group Policy inheritance has been blocked. Blocked inheritance means higher-level GPOs (including security baselines) do not apply to objects in that OU and its children.
Checks.Remediation: 1. In GPMC, OUs with blocked inheritance show a blue circle with exclamation icon. 2. Right-click the OU and uncheck 'Block Inheritance' unless specifically required. 3. Use GPO Enforced (No Override) at the domain/site level to push critical policies through blocked inheritance. 4. Document all intentionally blocked OUs and review quarterly.

Checks.PassMessage: No duplicate or excessive GPO links detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Duplicate GPO links detected - same GPO linked more than once to the same container
Container has more than 20 GPO links - may impact logon and startup performance
GPO link order issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks for duplicate GPO links (same GPO linked multiple times to the same OU) and OUs with excessive numbers of links. Duplicate links waste processing cycles and can cause unexpected policy application order.
Checks.Remediation: 1. In GPMC, review the Linked Group Policy Objects tab for each flagged OU. 2. Remove duplicate links by right-clicking and selecting Delete Link. 3. For OUs with many links, consolidate GPOs or reorganize the OU structure. 4. Consider using GPO Starter GPOs or merging multiple small GPOs to reduce link count.

EvaluationRules.Message: Cross-domain GPO links detected - GPOs from another domain are linked in this domain

Checks.Help: Detects GPOs from one domain linked to OUs in another domain in a multi-domain forest. Cross-domain GPO links create dependencies between domains and can cause policy application delays if the source domain DC is unreachable.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify cross-domain links in GPMC by examining GPO GUIDs vs. the source domain. 2. Recreate the GPO in the target domain with equivalent settings. 3. Update the OU link to point to the newly created local GPO. 4. Delete the cross-domain link after validating the replacement GPO applies correctly.
